title: Voting Rage and the Discipline of Showing Up Anyway
dek: Your body tightens when you hear the news—masseter clenched, shoulders creeping to your ears, the diaphragm tethered by a thick fog of fury. This is not pathology; this is biology speaking through clenched fists and restless nights. Rooted in the somatic traditions of Resmaa Menakem and the attentiveness of Thich Nhat Hanh, you learn how to hold your rage without shame and still step into the world, voting anyway.
bodyMarkdown:
## Voting Rage and the Discipline of Showing Up Anyway

It’s ten minutes before dawn. You lie awake in the familiar weight of your own body—shellacked with a low hum of dread that you wake up every morning now, since election season traipsed back into your bloodstream like a familiar ghost. That tightening in your masseter muscles pulls at the corners of your mouth, so taut it feels like the bone itself is grinding—your jaw is braced like a clenched fist refusing to release. The layers of fascia around your neck coil with tension, the levator scapulae knitting upward as if trying to hide behind your ears. You taste metal in your mouth, a coppery scrape out of place, and somewhere in your chest, right by the sternum, your heart feels trapped in a hot cage of anger—you can almost smell the sharp iron tang of blood mixing with sweat that leaks from the skin at the nape of your neck.

This is the politics of your body speaking in a raw, unsweetened dialect. Your body knows what the news won’t say: anger is not a failure, not a bad habit, nor a character flaw. It is your body’s immediate, somatic reaction to injustice, to the betrayal of collective care, to the grinding slow violence inflicted through laws and lies. This is not just politics—it is biology. It is real, flesh-based fury, triggered by a timeline so brutal you can trace it down the arch of your rib cage and feel it press against your diaphragm like a clenched fist. Maybe you catch it sometimes in your breath. Quick. Shallow. An uninvited tightness that lingers long after you drop your phone, after the screen has gone dark.

When you read the headlines—or worse, scroll through the silent scroll of social media—you feel the flash of heat in your trapezius muscle, that thick muscle guarding the back of your neck and shoulders. It’s the first to tighten, tightening so persistently that your gaze turns inward, tracking where this rage is living in your body. The tight little knots like marbles under your fingers. The deep, persistent ache that settles in your solar plexus, as if your body is trying to warn you, trying to prepare you for what’s next.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that rage lives in the body long before it makes it to the mouth or the ballots. And Karla McLaren’s work shows us that rage is a vital messenger, not a sin to be punished or hidden. Here is the discipline you didn’t sign up for but cannot avoid: showing up to this broken landscape anyway, with your clenched jaw softened only when you allow yourself the language of your body. When you let your fury be felt—fully, honestly, somatically—you reclaim it from being a weapon turned inward or a shadow you hide beneath.

So you breathe into that tightness. You press your palms against ribs that flare like defensive wings. You might reach for a cup of warm mugwort tea, knowing the bitter herb will untangle fascial fibres like no quick fix ever could. You remember the body remembers. This is how you start. Not by waiting to feel “ready” or clean or righteous. But by feeling everything—even that slow burn in your chest—because it insists you show up anyway.

## The Biology of Betrayal: Why the Body Reacts to Systemic Failure as a Physical Threat

You know this betrayal in your body before your mind registers the headline. It coils in the levator scapulae, that tense muscle scrambling up the back of your neck, a tightening that wakes you at 3 a.m., chest rising shallowly under ribs that want to expand but can’t. The world’s fallibility lands inside like a physical blow—systemic failure isn’t just news; it’s trauma knocking at your fascia, pounding the diaphragm that should be breathing deeply in sleep. It is not metaphor. Your body reads betrayal as a threat to survival, because it is. Whether it’s a broken promise by a leader, a fractured system that dismisses your humanity, or the slow erosion of trust you carried like an amulet, your nervous system doesn’t distinguish political collapse from predator threat.

The amygdala lights up like a flare in total darkness, jarring your parasympathetic nervous system into retreat. This is not a problem in need of rational solutions. It is pure biology hardwired for survival. When Thich Nhat Hanh says “the body knows,” he’s naming a truth your trapezius muscle—snapped rigid in protest—remembers intimately. This is not some abstract disappointment. It’s a breach in your social safety net that hits your psoas, the deep core muscle clutching your lower spine, tightening like a vise because the ground beneath you shifted without warning.

What burns here between your scapulae isn’t just anxiety; it’s a primal flash of betrayal, a signal from your vagus nerve that the air around you has turned toxic. Your jaw clenches—the masseter fires in a grind as the body bracing itself for more damage. Gabor Maté, whose work exposes the wounds we carry beneath our skin, reminds us that this physical reaction isn’t failure or weakness. It’s a message, a summons framed by biology and history. Your body is screaming: you cannot predict this betrayal, but you must respond. This tension is the muscle of readiness and refusal, the silent count that ticks beneath your ribs as the noon sky turns gray.

You might reach for herbs like lemon balm or skullcap, silent partners in your body’s fierce reclamation ritual, calming that fevered pulse below the sternum. If you touch the bitters of gentian root with your tongue, imagine that slow, grounding sink into the earth beneath your feet—because this betrayal is not just an idea, it is an event that your muscles and fascia catalog for generations. This is not pathology. It is biology, ancient as your DNA, as visceral as the weight of your own blood racing through veins that recall every crack in the system. And this is the sound your body makes before you find the words.

## The Body Remembers the History You Didn’t Sign Up For

It’s just past twilight, the air sticky with an early spring humidity that clings to your nape and slides beneath your collarbone. Your trapezius tightens—tiny knots like stones lodged in the muscle’s slow grind—because what you’re living isn’t just about today’s outrage. Resmaa Menakem tells us the body carries the traces of collective trauma like an unspoken language beneath skin and fascia. This isn’t metaphor. Your levator scapulae muscles, the ones that cradle your neck and shoulder blades, remember shapes of fear passed down before you ever registered the headlines. When you feel that familiar spike of panic or rage on voting night, it’s the echo of a narrative sprawling beyond your own wild heart.

Bessel van der Kolk takes it deeper, reminding you that trauma lives in the body in ways the mind can’t always reach. The diaphragm constricts as if caught in a silent chokehold, your breath shallow even though your lungs want to roll wide open like dawn breaking over an endless field. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your nervous system doesn’t just respond to the immediate—political betrayal, the taut hinge of fractured promises—it negotiates with centuries of oppression and displacement. The fight in you is ancestral, threaded through the psoas muscle, tied tight to your survival. 

You are not alone in your rage, even when you feel isolated under fluorescent lights or in crowded rooms filled with averted eyes. The somatic patterns you embody are a communal inheritance. Menakem, who walks deeply in the terrain of racialized trauma, teaches that healing this ancestral fury is less about fixing yourself and more about sitting with the tremble beneath your skin, the visceral charge in your limbs. The body stores what the history books won’t say aloud: generations of fury, grief, and resistance held in muscle and marrow.

You show up again because your body remembers the cost of absence—the silence of spirits who couldn’t speak up or be heard. Every clench of your jaw, every pulse in your temples, every breath that feels like a negotiation with fire is a discipline—an offering to that lineage. This isn’t about quieting your rage; it’s about learning to steward it without losing yourself to the endless swell of collective pain. The work is not neat; it is raw, unyielding, and sometimes, it smells like crushed sage and damp earth, like something old and ready to be untangled. Your body knows. It has always known.

## The Herbalist’s Intervention: Specific Nervines and Adaptogens for Acute Political Despair

Late at night, when the world collapses behind your eyelids and your diaphragm clenches like a fist beneath your ribs, the rage feels elemental—like thunder trapped in your solar plexus. The trapezius holds the tension, rigid and unyielding, telling you that your shoulders have carried this burden far too long. In these moments, herbs move through the body like gentle currents beneath the storm, calming the fissures of your nervous system, inviting your musculature to soften without surrendering your fire.

You recognize this isn’t weakness; it’s survival. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that presence begins with the body’s language—your jaw remembers the grit that won’t release, the masseter clenched against a world unraveling. So does the levator scapulae, pulling your neck into a subtle hunch, protecting, guarding, fighting. You reach for nervines and adaptogens—not as a dulling agent, but as a way to maintain your edge while letting the tension ease enough for you to show up again. Because the discipline you practice is not the denial of your fury; it’s its wise stewardship.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is a tender nervine, the herbal equivalent of a calming breath that sinks deep into the vagus nerve’s pathways. It doesn’t obliterate your anger; instead, it whispers to your system that you are still safe enough to feel it fully. The bitterness of skullcap, like rain on dry stone, slowly erodes the sharpest edges of acute despair, releasing the diaphragm from its clammy grip. Paired with lemon balm (Melissa officinalis), it invites a brightening warmth through your chest and throat—a softening that doesn’t ask you to forget, only to breathe into the storm.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) sits heavier in the body, a grounded adaptogen that helps your adrenal system recalibrate after skirmishes with electoral news cycles and social media spirals. It sinks into the psoas, untangling the chronic tension lodged there like old battleships in a harbor. The root’s earthy scent reminds you of damp soil after rain—of endurance, not surrender. Gabor Maté writes often on the body’s memory of trauma; this medicine supports the nervous system’s memory too, reminding it that rage can be contained, held, and mobilized without breaking you down.

In the thick of political despair, you might feel the tightness in your temporal fascia, pulses hammering behind your eyes. Magnolia bark (Magnolia officinalis) can be a quiet ally here. Its sedative qualities smooth the neural pathways, a slow, unhurried flood of calm that respects your fight rather than anesthetizing it. This is not pathology, but biology: your system calling for balance so you can return to the world—eyes open, heart ablaze, body steadied.

You dose these allies not recklessly, but with the respect due to medicine that talks to your bones and blood. They become part of your practice—the discipline of showing up anyway—an offering to the nerves betrayed by rage, a balm that lets you carry your fury without being carried away by it. These herbs do not promise quiet resignation; they promise endurance, the quiet, fierce kind that stays awake to the work—and the work stays awake to you.

## Staying with the Fire: The Discipline of Staying Present in Your Rage

You know that roar in your chest at midnight, right above your solar plexus, tightening like a fist clenched too long—raw and restless and demanding? That’s where Karla McLaren meets you, reminding you that this isn’t some problem to be chased away, but a vital message you need to listen to with your whole skin and skeleton. When your anger begins to clench the masseter muscles around your jaw, or squeeze the levator scapulae knots behind your neck, that’s your body saying, *stay with me.* Not because I’m comfortable, but because I’m real. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your anger is not a mistake or a moral failure; it’s your body’s deeply coded signal coming through like an urgent telegram in the dark, telling you that something is off, something needs your attention.  

Pema Chödrön’s teaching hangs in the air like the chill scent of winter pine, reminding you that sitting in the discomfort of anger without trying to flee it or smother it is, paradoxically, an act of kindness—otherwise, your body short-circuits into fight or freeze, and the toxicity festers like stagnant water in the pelvis or diaphragm. You can almost taste the tight, metallic tang of adrenal overload on your tongue when you try to distract or shut down the fire. But staying present with your anger—feeling it like the slow crushing press of your trapezius pressing the weight of the sky onto your shoulders at 3 a.m.—means cultivating a different relationship with this heat. It’s the discipline of showing up to your body’s story in real-time, even when every fiber of your psoas screams resistance.  

McLaren’s work reminds you that anger carries wisdom when you listen through it, not over it or under it. Her method—naming emotions like a friend you won’t turn your back on—gives you permission to slow down, to feel the density in your scapulae and sternum, the way your breath stutters and then catches in your diaphragm, and to say, *I see you here; you are not going to break me.* This disciplined presence is fierce; it’s the opposite of collapse. And it’s one of the most radical acts you can perform for yourself when voting day’s fury threatens to swallow you whole: showing up anyway, grounded in your bones, with a body that remembers peace is possible beneath the quake of your rage. You don’t need to believe the morning will bring calm, only that your body—this living, breathing, remembering vessel—can carry the fire until the sun rises again.

## The Discipline of Showing Up: Voting as Somatic Boundary and Harm Reduction

When you come to the voting booth, your body is already bristling with the aftermath of a thousand small betrayals—tight masseters from grinding teeth in meetings, the subtle coil of levator scapulae tightening at the base of your neck as you swallow another bitter truth. This is a moment when rage isn’t a problem you fix with a checkmark. It's the heavy, humming undercurrent running beside your thumb as it presses the ballot, a current that insists on being known, touched, respected.

Voting is not salvation. It is not the grand answer to this knot of fury in your solar plexus or the fatigue wetting your palms at dawn. It is the discipline of showing up anyway, a somatic boundary you draw against the chaos. When you cast a ballot, you are not pretending the world is just; you are not pausing rage with a flick of ink. Instead, you are a body setting a line in the dirt—this is the ground I claim today, in spite of the blade-edge of disappointment I wear like a second skin.

Think of voting as harm reduction, not heroism. You bring all that tight diaphragm tension and the ache in your trapezius—proof that your body has learned to brace—and you channel it into this act of boundary marking. You’re settling your ribs down, rocking the breath into the belly, even if only for a moment, creating a space where your anger doesn’t have to erupt, where it can coexist with persistence. Thich Nhat Hanh calls this “peaceful action.” It isn’t a contradiction; it’s a muscle conditioning, an inner resilience built on showing up when your whole nervous system wants to flee.

You don’t have to feel hopeful to vote. You just have to feel the ground beneath your feet. You let the anger be the fuel that gets you out the door, the heat that warms your stiff joints, the fierce clarity that says, *I will not be erased.* This is how you honor the biology of your rage without letting it consume you. You show up, you draw the line, and you breathe out, knowing the work continues long after the polls close.

## Embodied Practice: Grounding the Fury Before You Vote

Before you leave the house, or while you stand in line, take a moment to honor the fire. This is not about calming down; it’s about anchoring the heat so it doesn’t burn you up from the inside.

Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Feel the solid earth beneath your shoes. Notice the tension in your jaw—the masseter muscles locked tight. Don’t force them to relax. Instead, place your fingertips gently on your jawline. Acknowledge the grip. Say to yourself, *I feel you holding on.* 

Now, bring your attention to your diaphragm. Take a breath that doesn’t try to be deep or perfect, just a breath that meets the tightness where it is. As you exhale, imagine sending the heat from your chest down through your legs, past your knees, and into the ground. The earth can hold this fire. It has held the fury of generations before you.

If you need to, clench your fists as tight as you can for three seconds. Feel the energy gather in your forearms, the readiness in your biceps. Then, slowly, deliberately, open your hands. Let your palms face forward. You are not dropping your anger; you are simply opening your hands to the work ahead. You are ready.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like my anger is too big for my body. What do I do when it feels overwhelming?**  
A: When the rage feels like it’s going to split your ribs, you don’t try to contain it all at once. You give it a physical outlet. You let the body move. Shake your hands out, stomp your feet, let the trapezius release its grip through motion. The body needs to know it’s allowed to express the sheer volume of this feeling. It’s not pathology; it’s biology demanding a release valve.

**Q: Can I use herbs if I’m already taking medication for anxiety or depression?**  
A: Always consult with a clinical herbalist or your healthcare provider before mixing herbs with pharmaceuticals. Herbs like St. John’s Wort or Ashwagandha can interact with certain medications. But remember, the goal isn’t to medicate the anger away; it’s to support the nervous system so you can hold the anger without breaking.

**Q: What if voting feels like a betrayal of my own values because the choices are so bleak?**  
A: Your body knows the difference between a perfect choice and a necessary boundary. Voting in a broken system often feels like a compromise, and that friction lives in the psoas, in the gut. Acknowledge that friction. You aren’t voting to save your soul; you’re voting as an act of harm reduction, a somatic boundary against further violence. It’s okay if it feels heavy. You carry it anyway.

**Q: How do I keep from burning out after the election is over?**  
A: The discipline of showing up doesn’t end at the ballot box. It shifts. You return to the practices that tend to your nervous system. You drink the bitter teas, you rub the tension out of your levator scapulae, you sit with the grief that often follows the rage. You remember that this is a long, generational work, and your body needs rest just as much as it needs fire.
